chunk_summary: Adolescent girls face high risk of iron deficiency and anemia post-menarche due to dietary factors, heavy menstrual flow, and poor absorption. National guidelines recommend prevention via WIFS and structured treatment pathways for diagnosed anemia based on hemoglobin levels.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional risks associated with menarche, including iron deficiency and anemia.
Learn the national guidelines for anemia prevention and treatment in Indian adolescents.
Identify risk factors such as heavy menstrual flow, dietary restrictions, and hemoglobinopathies.
Apply structured treatment pathways for anemia based on severity and hemoglobin levels.
key_facts: Anemia is common in Indian adolescent girls, with hemoglobin <11 g/dL indicating mild anemia.
National guidelines recommend WIFS (weekly iron–folic acid supplementation) for prevention.
Moderate anemia (Hb 8–10.9 g/dL) requires daily elemental iron for 3 months with reassessment.
Severe anemia (Hb <8 g/dL) warrants urgent referral for stabilization.
Therapeutic dosing for pediatrics is often 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron.
reference_quote: India’s national guideline for adolescents 10–19 years categorizes mild anemia around hemoglobin 11–11.9 g/dL and moderate anemia 8–10.9 g/dL, treated with daily elemental iron for about 3 months with hemoglobin recheck; hemoglobin <8 g/dL warrants urgent referral for evaluation and stabilization.
